_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Gaston County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.
display_name: Gaston County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat was at Dallas until 1911 when it moved to Gastonia. The Dallas courthouse was not destroyed but ceased to serve as the county courthouse.
Served until the county seat transferred to Gastonia. Records moved to the new courthouse.

record_losses.cause: None — no major courthouse fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Gaston County was formed from Lincoln County in 1846 and has suffered no major courthouse fire. Records begin at formation and run continuously. The county seat moved from Dallas to Gastonia in 1911, and records were transferred intact. For pre-1846 records, Lincoln County (Lincolnton) is the parent county.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Gaston County's civil records are intact from 1846, but the county's real genealogical richness lies in the mill company records. If {ancestor_name} worked in a Gaston County textile mill, the company payroll and housing records at NC State Archives may contain more specific information than the courthouse itself.
record_losses.source: Cope, Robert F., and Manly Wade Wellman. The County of Gaston: Two Centuries of a North Carolina Region. Gastonia: Gaston County Historical Society, 1961.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1846 deeds for land within current Gaston County boundaries are in Lincoln County records at Lincolnton.
deeds.repository: Gaston County Register of Deeds, Gastonia; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from formation
wills_estates.gaps: No significant gaps. Pre-1846 wills for this area are in Lincoln County records.
wills_estates.repository: Gaston County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county formation
marriages.gaps: Pre-1846 marriages are in Lincoln County records.
marriages.repository: Gaston County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Gaston County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Gastonia city records may predate county registration.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates

substitute_sources.source_type: Textile mill company records — payroll, housing, employment
substitute_sources.description: Gaston County had over 100 textile mills at its peak, making it one of the most concentrated textile manufacturing centers in the world. Mill company records — including payroll ledgers, company housing assignments, employee rosters, and mill village church membership rolls — survive for several major operations. These records are held at NC State Archives, Gaston County Museum, and in some cases remain with successor companies.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives (multiple mill company record groups); Gaston County Museum of Art and History, Dallas; UNC-Charlotte Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Extraordinarily valuable for identifying mill worker families. Payroll records provide names, dates of employment, wages, and sometimes family relationships. Company housing records show which family occupied which house and when, creating a residential record more detailed than the decennial census. For the tens of thousands of families who passed through Gaston County mills, these records may be the richest genealogical source available.
substitute_sources.source: Glass, Brent D. The Textile Industry in North Carolina: A History. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1992. Pope, Liston. Millhands and Preachers: A Study of Gastonia. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1942.

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for the territory that became Gaston County are found in Lincoln County (Lincolnton) before 1846. Lincoln County was formed in 1779 and has good record survival. The Scots-Irish and German settler families who populated what is now Gaston County appear in Lincoln County deeds, wills, and court minutes.
substitute_sources.repository: Lincoln County Register of Deeds, Lincolnton; NC State Archives microfilm; FamilySearch
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Gaston County family research before 1846. The German and Scots-Irish settlement patterns visible in Lincoln County records carry directly into early Gaston County.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Before 1850, families in present-day Gaston County territory were enumerated under Lincoln County. From 1850 onward, under Gaston County. Manufacturing schedules (1850–1880) are valuable given the early textile development.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families across the 1846 county formation. Manufacturing census schedules provide early industrial context.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Gaston and Lincoln Counties, NC.

substitute_sources.description: The 1929 Loray Mill Strike in Gastonia was a nationally significant labor event. Arrest records, court transcripts, newspaper coverage, NTWU organizer correspondence, and FBI surveillance files document the workers and community members involved. These records name hundreds of Gaston County mill families.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; National Archives (FBI files); Millican Memorial Library, Gastonia; Southern Labor Archives, Georgia State University
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for identifying specific mill worker families and their circumstances in 1929. Court records and arrest lists name workers and their addresses, occupations, and family connections.
substitute_sources.source: Salmond, John A. Gastonia 1929: The Story of the Loray Mill Strike. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1995.

narrative_hooks: Gaston County's courthouse records start in 1846, but the county's real genealogical treasure is the mill company records — if {ancestor_name} worked in a Gastonia textile mill, the payroll ledgers at NC State Archives may show exactly when {pronoun_subject} arrived, what {pronoun_subject} earned, and which company house {pronoun_subject} lived in.
If {ancestor_name}'s family was in Gaston County before 1846, the paper trail leads back to Lincoln County at Lincolnton — same land, same families, different courthouse.

Before 1913, no North Carolina county required birth or death registration, so the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} in Gaston County is normal — mill village church rolls, company records, and census schedules fill the gap.

Gaston County had over 100 textile mills within its borders — if {ancestor_name} moved between mills, the company records may track that movement more precisely than any other source, showing a family that appeared at one plant, disappeared, and resurfaced at another across the county.

If {ancestor_name} was in Gastonia during the 1929 Loray Mill Strike, the arrest records, court transcripts, and newspaper coverage may document {pronoun_possessive} involvement in one of the most dramatic labor conflicts in American history.
source_references: Cope, Robert F., and Manly Wade Wellman. The County of Gaston: Two Centuries of a North Carolina Region. Gastonia: Gaston County Historical Society, 1961.
Glass, Brent D. The Textile Industry in North Carolina: A History. Raleigh: NC Division of Archives and History, 1992.
Pope, Liston. Millhands and Preachers: A Study of Gastonia. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1942.
Salmond, John A. Gastonia 1929: The Story of the Loray Mill Strike. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1995.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
